source_title: #857: How to Simplify Your Life in 2026 — New Tips from Maria Popova, Morgan Housel, Cal Newport, Craig Mod, and Debbie Millman

paragraphs.speaker: tim
paragraphs.name: Tim Ferriss
paragraphs.text: Many of us feel like we're drowning in complexity—too many apps, decision fatigue, overwhelm. So I hit pause to ask: What are one to three decisions that could dramatically simplify my life in 2026? I invited five listener favorites—Maria Popova, Morgan Housel, Cal Newport, Craig Mod, and Debbie Millman—to share how they've simplified their lives.

paragraphs.speaker: maria
paragraphs.name: Maria Popova
paragraphs.text: I adopted what I call the cherish quotient. I decided to stop giving my time to people whose company and conversation I don't absolutely cherish—not just like or appreciate, but cherish. As Annie Dillard wrote, how we spend our days is of course how we spend our lives. Every middling hour is a step toward a middling life. Anything you give your time and attention to should roil with the magma of yes.

paragraphs.speaker: maria
paragraphs.name: Maria Popova
paragraphs.text: I stopped using auto-responders or apologizing for how long it takes me to return a text. The moment you begin apologizing for how you manage your time, you are essentially apologizing for your priorities, which means apologizing for your life. We need a basic faith that everyone is doing the best with the resources they have and the demands their life places upon them.

paragraphs.speaker: morgan
paragraphs.name: Morgan Housel
paragraphs.text: My entire net worth is a house, cash, Vanguard index funds, and shares of Markel where I'm on the board of directors. It is hard to imagine a more simple investing philosophy. The fewer decisions you have to make as an investor, the better you're going to do over the course of your life. If I can be an average investor for an above-average period of time, I'm going to outperform the huge majority of investors.

paragraphs.speaker: morgan
paragraphs.name: Morgan Housel
paragraphs.text: A really good heuristic for your relationship with information is read more history and fewer forecasts. When you immerse yourself in history, you become familiar with psychological trends that repeat over and over again. Once you understand those big trends, your ability to filter the news is much stronger. As Kelly Hayes says, when you haven't engaged with history, everything feels unprecedented.

paragraphs.speaker: cal
paragraphs.name: Cal Newport
paragraphs.text: My body cannot handle busyness. When I have too many things to do, I get anxious and stressed out. I need autonomy and space to work on my own terms. I've learned that no basically has to be my default answer to opportunities. Whatever rule I came up with for what's good enough, too many things satisfied that rule. I would cycle between being overloaded and then angrily saying no to everything.

paragraphs.speaker: cal
paragraphs.name: Cal Newport
paragraphs.text: Now I'm really only agreeing if it's something I can bring my family to or if it's cool and super convenient. The goal with simplifying isn't to avoid bad things—it's trying to hit an ideal lifestyle. For me, my ideal lifestyle isn't too busy. I'm missing out on cool experiences, but that's okay.

paragraphs.speaker: cal
paragraphs.name: Cal Newport
paragraphs.text: I had two major lives going on—my career as a writer and my career as a professor doing distributed algorithm theory. It was too complicated. The discovery I had is that my book Deep Work was about technology, my next books were about technology, my podcast covered technology. I realized these aren't two different worlds. I put a pause on distributed algorithm theory to say all of my effort is aimed at the same thing: thinking and writing about technology and its impacts on human flourishing.

paragraphs.speaker: craig
paragraphs.name: Craig Mod
paragraphs.text: Cutting out alcohol—easily the lowest energy, biggest impact simplification of my life. I struggled mightily with alcohol abuse in my 20s. Nothing made things more complicated than this destructive relationship. For me, the catalyst was finding deep meaning in my work. Every drink I took, I saw and felt in my bones as taking away from that work. That alone was enough for me to say no easily, consistently. That was about 18 years ago.

paragraphs.speaker: craig
paragraphs.name: Craig Mod
paragraphs.text: Starting therapy in earnest almost nine years ago was one of the simplest decisions with the biggest impact. Therapy, when done really well, cuts to the bone in a clarifying way. It calls out all the little voices you carry around in your head. In demystifying yourself and clarifying who you really are, you are paradoxically freer, less limited than ever. Therapy cleans the waters and allows you to move forward in simpler ways.

paragraphs.speaker: craig
paragraphs.name: Craig Mod
paragraphs.text: Almost nothing in my life has paid bigger dividends than stopping my waffling around and committing to craft. I'm a writer—the end. The more I've doubled down on that choice, the simpler my life has become. Everyone I love and respect can be traced back almost one-to-one to the commitment to the craft of writing. Instead of being a jack-of-fifty trades, I chose one trade to commit to.

paragraphs.speaker: debbie
paragraphs.name: Debbie Millman
paragraphs.text: In 2016, I turned down a job offer to become CEO of the company where I'd worked for
